notes: Founded 1956 importing Willys Jeeps; began local assembly of the 'Shahbaz' Jeep in 1959 (first Iranian-produced SUV); renamed Pars Khodro after 1979-80 nationalization of its later GM joint venture

start_year: Iran National Company (founded Aug 1962 by Ahmad and Mahmud Khayami, initial capital 100,000,000 Rials)
source: 1966: Rootes Group licensed Iran National to produce the Rootes Arrow (Hillman Hunter); production began 13 May 1967 at 6,000 units/yr, badged 'Paykan' (Persian for Arrow). Local content rose from near-zero to ~44% by 1979 and ~98% in later production. 1967-2005 total Paykan output exceeded 2.2 million units. Foreign partner ownership changed hands twice (Rootes to Chrysler UK 1967, Chrysler UK to Peugeot/renamed Talbot 1978) without Iran National losing domestic management control per Encyclopaedia Iranica
column9: Encyclopaedia Iranica IRAN NATIONAL COMPANY; aronline.co.uk; Wikipedia Paykan; WebSearch aggregation

notes: Iran National ran a parallel license agreement with Mercedes-Benz of Germany for assembly of buses and minibuses (semi-knocked-down parts) alongside its Rootes/Chrysler passenger-car licence -- both licences held simultaneously by the same Iranian firm, illustrating the multi-partner-licensing model common in pre-1979 Iranian industrial JVs

venture_name: Khawar Industrial Group (-> Iran Khodro Diesel)
iranian_partner: Iranian investors (name not identified this round)
end_year_or_status: ongoing (post-1999 merged into Iran Khodro Diesel)
notes: Separate Mercedes-Benz TRUCK assembly-under-license venture (distinct from the Iran National bus/minibus licence above), founded 1966 as Khawar Industrial Group; merged with Iran Khodro's bus/midibus lines in 1999 to form Iran Khodro Diesel, which continued Daimler licensing relationships (with interruptions/disputes under sanctions) well past the Islamic Revolution

June 1972: GM signs deal forming General Motors Iran Ltd.; produced the Opel Commodore under license 1974-1976 badged 'Chevrolet Royale'. Nationalized after the 1979 revolution and renamed Pars Khodro in 1980, absorbing/succeeding the earlier Jeep Iran Trading Company lineage
